region_of_origin: Global (North America, Europe, New Zealand, Southeast Asia)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of raw venison is approximately 85 g (3 oz), equivalent to a palm-sized portion before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Deer, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17163)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 116 kcal, protein 21.5 g, fat 2.66 g, carbohydrate 0.0 g) and micronutrients (calcium 7 mg, iron 2.9 mg, cholesterol 18 mg) are sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id F06080003, confidence 0.95) as the primary regional source. Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, sodium, potassium, B vitamins, minerals) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17163 for raw deer meat, which is an approved secondary global source. Values are consistent across both approved sources.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; GI concept does not apply
gi_source.source_match_notes: Raw venison contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g as reported by Singapore HPB Foodo (cr_id F06080003) and confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17163. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI value exists or is needed for a zero-carbohydrate animal protein. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. Per Project Veritas v2 rules for low/zero-carbohydrate foods: glycemic_index is set to null (not 0), gi_category is set to not_applicable.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (54 mg/100 g) and low saturated fat (1.06 g/100 g) with a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (potassium 335 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Raw venison is a suitable lean protein for hypertension management; prepare by grilling, roasting, or stewing without added salt, and avoid high-sodium marinades or curing agents that would significantly raise the sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar with high protein (21.5 g/100 g) supporting postprandial blood glucose stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw venison contains no carbohydrate and will not raise postprandial blood glucose; it is an excellent lean protein choice for type 2 diabetes management, provided it is not prepared with high-sugar marinades, glazes, or breading that would add carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (2.66 g/100 g), low saturated fat (1.06 g/100 g), and low cholesterol (18 mg/100 g) relative to other red meats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Venison is one of the leanest red meats available and is appropriate for cardiovascular disease management when consumed in moderate portions and prepared without added saturated fats; its low saturated fat and cholesterol profile compare favourably to beef and lamb.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid (omega-6) content in red/game meat may amplify inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Venison is leaner than beef or lamb but remains a red game meat containing arachidonic acid; RA patients should limit consumption to once weekly, pair with anti-inflammatory foods (omega-3-rich fish, turmeric, leafy greens), and avoid deep-frying which generates advanced glycation end-products that worsen joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deer/venison, raw, is the uncooked lean dark red meat obtained from various cuts of deer (Cervidae family), presented without bone. It displays a deep burgundy colour with a firm, dense texture characteristic of wild or farmed game meat. Venison is one of the leanest red meats available, providing 21.5 g of high-quality complete protein per 100 g with only 2.66 g of total fat and 1.06 g of saturated fat, making it nutritionally superior to beef and lamb in terms of fat content. It contains zero carbohydrates and zero sugar, making it ideal for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. The energy density is low at 116 kcal per 100 g. Venison is a rich source of B vitamins including B12 (2.51 mcg), niacin (6.38 mg), B6 (0.54 mg), and riboflavin (0.49 mg), as well as minerals including iron (2.9 mg), zinc (2.65 mg), phosphorus (199 mg), and potassium (335 mg). Its sodium content is naturally low at 54 mg per 100 g. Clinically, raw venison is suitable for hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease management due to its low sodium, low saturated fat, zero carbohydrate, and high lean protein profile. It is consumed globally and is particularly popular in North America, Europe, New Zealand, and parts of Southeast Asia. Raw venison must be cooked to a safe internal temperature before consumption to eliminate foodborne pathogens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in raw venison?
Is deer meat good for diabetes?
Can I eat venison on a keto diet?
Is deer meat good for high blood pressure?
What nutrients are in raw deer meat?
Is venison suitable for heart disease patients?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id F06080003, confidence 0.95) as the primary approved regional source. Secondary nutrients cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17163 for raw deer meat, an approved secondary global source. Values are consistent across both approved sources.
notes: carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as venison is a pure animal muscle protein with no carbohydrate content. sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17163. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal muscle food product — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy NDB 17163). vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: vitamin D data for raw deer meat is not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17163 or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of April 2026; both approved sources were consulted and do not provide this value. vitamin_e_mg set to -1: vitamin E data for raw deer meat is not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17163 or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of April 2026; both approved sources were consulted and do not provide this value. Halal status is not confirmed as deer slaughter method varies by source; consumers should verify halal certification independently. Venison must be cooked to a safe internal temperature (minimum 74 degrees C for whole cuts) before consumption to eliminate foodborne pathogens including E. coli and Salmonella. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03411433 and SNOMED CT code 226934003 are best-match defensible assignments; FoodEx2 code A0CKP is the standard EFSA code for venison/deer meat. GI is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100 g); glycemic_index is set to null per Project Veritas v2 rules for zero-carbohydrate foods.
